title: How To Future-Proof Yourself In The Age of AI
description: In this video, Sandeep unpacks how to future-proof your life in the age of AI. Drawing on 25 years of experience as a tech CEO, investor, and board member, he explores why the skills that set humans apart—like asking better questions, earning trust, making tough calls under uncertainty, and reinventing yourself—are more critical than ever. Instead of competing with machines, you'll discover how to double down on what makes you uniquely human and position yourself to thrive no matter how fast technology evolves.
transcript:
AI is taking over our lives and could be
taking over our jobs. So what do we do?
I have spent 20 years working in tech
and AI as a CEO, board member, investor,
building billiondoll companies. These
are five ways to futureproof your life
in the age of AI. Let's begin. Number
one, the question is usually the answer.
MIT researchers found that 50% of good
AI results come not from better models
but from asking better questions. This
is true especially on complex realw
world tasks. But here's the real
surprise. When users let AI generate
those same questions, the results
actually got worse. Humans are better
than AI in that one area. That's the
upside of human intuition. Here's a real
world story. Reed Hastings was the CEO

series project that he loved. The
algorithm analyzed countless viewing
patterns and engagement metrics of
existing viewers and arrived at a very
very clear conclusion. The show was
going to flop. But Hastings sense
something the data couldn't. a cultural
shift toward a darker serialized
storytelling. So, he took a gamble and
decided to greenlight the project.
Anyway, the show was House of Cards. It

The company completely transformed from

making decisions based on our intuition.
Action items for us. First, when you're
using AI, ask what data isn't showing
yet. Find your human edge by scanning
for opportunities and risks that sit
outside the model's frame of reference.
In other words, hone your intuition.
Second, spend 80% of your thinking time
on the question, not the answer. What
assumptions are baked into your data?
What signals might be missing? And
third, get close to your market. Talk to
your customers. Observe your audience.
Experience what they're experiencing.
Because most cultural shifts and
emerging trends show up in real life
long before they show up in AI models.
Next is number two. Trust is earned by
humans, not AI. Mount Sinai Hospital
built AI that can diagnose some lung

the twist. Patients still want to hear
from the doctor. When the doctor
explicitly says, "I looked at the data

2008. All tech, all algorithms, and all

not predict or stop the most severe

Millions lost their jobs. Families lost

at that point at the epicenter of
collapse. Our global economy runs on
trust. It's the inevitable architecture
that holds everything up. So when trust
collapses, no AI model can fix it.
Humans have to be the model humans
trust. So you can try these three things
to become more trustworthy. First, do
what you said you'll do. As Steven CVY
said, you can't talk your way out of
something you walked your way into.
Humans value humans who say what they'll
do and then do what they say. Sounds so
obvious, right? But maybe that's why
most of us easily forget about it. Your
words do matter. Be reliable and you'll

deliver more. Simple, but hard to
remember that when everybody is
overselling. And third, apologize when
you're wrong. Mistakes don't destroy
trust. Denial, and defensiveness, do own
your mistakes early. It restores
credibility. Number three is next, and
it's one that most people struggle with,
making decisions under uncertainty.
Harvard University did a 10-year study
of 17,000 executives and found that top
performers share one very interesting
trait. They excel at decisive actions
under uncertainty. When stakes are high
and variables are constantly changing,
that's when it matters. A US bank called
Silicon Valley Bank or SVB served a lot
of tech companies and startups. And in
2023, SVB collapsed in real time. And in
one day, $42 billion evaporated from the
bank. Interest rates were high. Startups
pulled their money. Algorithms did not
know what to do. And neither did the
regulators. But one person saw the real
danger. It was Jamie Diamond. He knew
that the failure of that one crucial

was ticking. The problem was getting
bigger by the hour. He had no time to
analyze every single variable. He picked

ingest $30 billion into SVB to stop the

decisiveness when the problem itself was
changing its shape. The thing about
decision-m is that it requires five
different skills simultaneously.
analytical, emotional, cultural, moral,

So, here are the three things you can
try to hone your decision-making skills.
First, follow the 70% rule. Make
decisions when you have 70% confidence
in data. Most of us wait for that 100%
clarity and certainty that never comes.
And that's why when we make decisions,
it's already too late. There is no 100%

health, creativity, and act on it
without analyzing for hours. And
finally, record how it turned out. Keep
a decision log. Write down what you
knew, what you felt, and why you acted.
That will help you make better decisions

number four, The Importance of

Because when AI systems fail at 3:00

After 30 days, meditators show 32%

human. We need to revive the forgotten

when your mind is spinning around and

framework from cognitive behavior
therapy. is called stop. S is for slow
down. Stabilize your attention. Speed is
where mistakes happen. T is for take a

observe not outside but inside. Notice

with intention, not impulse. Respond
when you're clear, not when you trigger.
All of this is easier said than done. I

It's the new reality in every profession

dead. The new one is a lifelong journey.
In that journey, the goal isn't going to
be how to compete with AI. These
machines will learn at exponential

won't forget things. Instead, we have to
focus on mastering the skills that AI
won't have, that AI can't have. In that
world dominated by artificial

like a house built on four human
intelligence pillars. Pillar number one,
physical intelligence. Skills that
require physical expertise, presence,
and intuition in the messy realworld
environments. Think like trade skills,
emergency response, live performance,
field engineering, areas where physical

collaboration, giving more than

evenness of mind in the face of life's

and failure. In the era of AI, it means
staying steady through the wild swings
of markets, news, technology, public
